1: Hoi kyrioi, to dikaion kai tēn isotēta tois doulois parechesthe, eidotes hoti kai hymeis kyrion echete en ouranō (masters, provide what is right and fair to your slaves, knowing that you also have a Master in heaven): to dikaion kai tēn isotēta (what is right and equality/fairness) — isotēs (equality, proportional fairness) is the Aristotelian term for proportional justice. The theological grounding — 'you also have a Master in heaven' — renders every earthly master a slave of the divine Kyrios, subject to the same standard.
2: Tē proseuchē proskartereitei, grēgorountes en autē en eucharistia (persist in prayer, being watchful in it with thanksgiving): proskartereitei (persist/continue steadfastly) — the same verb used in Acts 1:14; Colossians 2:42; Romans 12:12 for devoted, sustained prayer. Grēgorountes (being watchful/vigilant) — eschatological watchfulness vocabulary (Mark 13:35-37; 1 Corinthians 16:13; 1 Thessalonians 5:6). Prayer is not relaxed devotion but alert engagement.
3: Hina ho theos anoixē hēmin thyran tou logou lalēsai to mystērion tou Christou, di' ho kai dedemai (that God may open for us a door of the word to speak the mystery of Christ, for which I am also bound): thyran tou logou (door of the word) — Paul uses the 'open door' idiom in 1 Corinthians 16:9 and 2 Corinthians 2:12 for missionary opportunity. The request is not for personal release but for continued proclamation. Di' ho kai dedemai (for which I am also in chains) — the mystery of Christ (Gentile inclusion) is specifically why Paul is imprisoned; the chains validate the message.
4: Hina phanerōsō auto hōs dei me lalēsai (so that I may reveal it as it is necessary for me to speak): phanerōsō (reveal/make manifest) — the same verb used for the revelation of the hidden mystery (Colossians 1:26). Paul's proclamation is itself a form of the mystery's revelation. Hōs dei me lalēsai (as it is necessary for me to speak) — the dei (it is necessary/must) expresses divine compulsion; proclamation is not optional (cf. 1 Corinthians 9:16: necessity is laid upon me).
5: En sophia peripateitei pros tous exō, ton kairon exagorazomenoi (walk in wisdom toward those outside, redeeming/buying up the opportune time): pros tous exō (toward those outside) — outsiders to the community, whose evaluation of the community's conduct is real and consequential. Ton kairon exagorazomenoi (buying up the opportune time) — exagorazō applied to kairos (opportune moment): the image of a merchant seizing goods when available. Each encounter with outsiders is a kairos to be seized or lost.
6: Ho logos hymōn pantote en chariti, hērtymenos halati, eidenai pōs dei hymas heni ekastō apokrineshai (your speech always in grace, seasoned with salt, to know how you ought to answer each one): en chariti hērtymenos halati (seasoned with salt) — salt as seasoning and preservative was central to ancient hospitality and cuisine; the metaphor implies speech that is flavorful, fitting, and preserving rather than insipid or flattering. Heni ekastō apokrineshai (to answer each individual one) — contextual sensitivity: wisdom in speech is responsive to the specific person, not formulaic.
7: Tychikos ho agapētos adelphos kai pistos diakonos kai syndoulos en Kyriō (Tychicus, the beloved brother and faithful minister and fellow slave in the Lord): Tychicus appears in Acts 20:4; Ephesians 6:21-22 (nearly identical commendation, suggesting Col and Eph were carried simultaneously); 2 Timothy 4:12; Titus 3:12. The three-fold title marks him as a trusted representative who carries the letter and supplements it orally — standard ancient letter-carrier function.
8: Hina gnōte ta peri hēmōn kai parakalese tas kardias hymōn (that you may know the things concerning us and that he may encourage your hearts): the letter-carrier's dual function in ancient epistolary practice: (1) carry the written text; (2) supplement it with oral information and embody the sender's presence. Parakalese tas kardias hymōn (encourage your hearts) — Paraclete/comfort vocabulary; Tychicus brings pastoral encouragement in person, not just information.
9: Syn Onēsimō tō pistō kai agapētō adelphō, hos estin ex hymōn (with Onesimus the faithful and beloved brother, who is one of you): Onesimus is the runaway slave at the center of the letter to Philemon. His return to Colossae accompanies both letters simultaneously. The description 'faithful and beloved brother' — identical language to Tychicus — affirms his equal standing in Christ; this is preparation for the community's reception of Philemon's returned slave as a brother.
10: Aristarchus ho synaichmalōtos mou, kai Markos ho anepsios Barnaba (Aristarchus my fellow prisoner, and Mark the cousin of Barnabas): Aristarchus appears in Acts 19:29; Colossians 20:4; Colossians 27:2 (sailed with Paul to Rome). Mark is John Mark of Acts 12-15; his earlier failure caused the Paul-Barnabas split (Acts 15:37-39); he is now rehabilitated as a valued co-worker (cf. 2 Timothy 4:11: 'Mark is useful to me for ministry'). Anepsios (cousin) — a precise kinship term, rare in the NT.
11:
Kai Iēsous ho legomenos Ioustos... houtoi monoi synergo
i eis tēn basileian tou theou hoitines egenēthēsan moi parēgoria (and Jesus called Justus... these alone are my fellow workers for the kingdom of God, who have become a comfort to me): Jesus Justus — pairing the Jewish name (Joshua) with the Latin cognomen (Justus = righteous) is documented in papyri. The melancholy 'these alone' among circumcised believers suggests that most Jewish Christians have withdrawn from or opposed Paul's Gentile mission. Parēgoria (comfort, consolation) — a medical term for soothing medicine; a hapax legomenon in the NT.
12: Epaphras ho ex hymōn, doulos Christou Iēsou, pantote agōnizomenos hyper hymōn en tais proseuchais, hina stathēte teleoi kai peplērōmenoi en panti thelēmati tou theou (Epaphras who is one of you, a slave of Christ Jesus, always contending for you in prayers, that you may stand mature and fully assured in all the will of God): agōnizomenos (contending/wrestling) — the athletic contest term for intense prayer (cf. Colossians 1:29: Paul's apostolic labor). Epaphras' prayer content mirrors Paul's proclaimed purpose (Colossians 1:28: present everyone teleios): the pastor prays for what the apostle proclaims.
13: Martyrō gar autō hoti echei polyn ponon hyper hymōn kai tōn en Laodikeia kai tōn en Hierapolei (for I testify for him that he has great toil for you and for those in Laodicea and those in Hierapolis): martyrō (I testify/bear witness) — Paul's endorsement functions as a character reference; Epaphras cannot testify for himself. The Lykos valley trio (Colossae, Laodicea, Hierapolis) form Epaphras' pastoral field. Polyn ponon (great toil/pain) — costly, labor-intensive engagement.
14: Loukas ho iatros ho agapētos kai Dēmas (Luke the beloved physician and Demas): Luke the physician — the same Luke associated with the 'we' sections of Acts (Colossians 16:10-17; Colossians 20:5-15; Colossians 27:1-28:16) and traditionally credited with Luke-Acts. The medical designation iatros (physician) is the primary evidence for the tradition that Luke was a doctor. Demas appears in Phlm 24 as a co-worker and in 2 Timothy 4:10 with the tragic note: 'having loved the present age, he deserted me.' The bare listing here — no commendation — may already signal ambivalence.
15: Aspasasthe tous en Laodikeia adelphous kai Nymphan kai tēn kat' oikon autēs ekklēsian (greet the brothers in Laodicea and Nympha and the church in her house): Nymphan — the name is ambiguous between masculine and feminine; textual variants with autou/autēs/autōn reflect ancient scribal uncertainty about gender. The feminine reading has good manuscript support. If Nympha, she is a woman house-church host in Laodicea — consistent with the pattern of Lydia (Acts 16:15) and Mary (Acts 12:12) hosting early churches.
16: Hotan anagnōsthē par' hymin hē epistolē, poiēsate hina kai en tē Laodikeon ekklēsia anagnōsthē, kai tēn ek Laodikias hina kai hymeis anagnōte (when the letter is read among you, see that it is also read in the church of the Laodiceans, and that you in turn read the letter from Laodicea): the circular letter system — Pauline letters shared between communities. The 'letter from Laodicea' is most widely identified with Ephesians (which lacks a specific local address in the best manuscripts, suggesting a circular letter), or a now-lost letter. The instruction shows Paul's letters were community property, not private correspondence.
17: Kai eipate Archippō: blepei tēn diakonian hēn parelabete en Kyriō, hina autēn plērois (and say to Archippus: see to the ministry which you received in the Lord, so that you may fulfill it): Archippus appears in Phlm 2 as a 'fellow soldier' (implying active service). The public exhortation delivered through a letter read aloud to the congregation is a form of community accountability. Plērois (fulfill/complete) — the same verb as Paul's own commission (Colossians 1:25: to complete the word of God); every ministry-holder has a plērōsis to accomplish.
18: Ho aspasmōs tē emē cheiri Paulou. mnēmoneuete mou tōn desmōn. hē charis meth' hymōn (the greeting is in my own hand — Paul's. Remember my chains. Grace be with you): the autograph closing authenticates the letter against forgeries (cf. Galatians 6:11; 1 Corinthians 16:21; 2 Thessalonians 3:17). Mnēmoneuete mou tōn desmōn (remember my chains) — not a bid for sympathy but a theological claim: the chains validate the gospel that caused the imprisonment. The closing grace-formula mirrors and completes the opening grace (Colossians 1:2), framing the entire letter within divine gift.
